---
title: "Regularization"
subtitle: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting --- Lecture 8"
author: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting"
institute: "University of Nebraska at Omaha"
date: "Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, emory-clean.scss]
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.05
    logo: ""
    footer: "BSAD 8310 | University of Nebraska at Omaha | Spring 2026"
    embed-resources: true
bibliography: ../Bibliography_base.bib
csl: https://www.zotero.org/styles/chicago-author-date
---

## Lecture 8: Outline

1. Motivation: Why Regularization?
2. Ridge Regression (L2)
3. LASSO Regression (L1)
4. Elastic Net
5. Tuning $\lambda$ via Cross-Validation
6. Application to Forecasting

---

# Motivation: Why Regularization?

OLS breaks down when predictors are many, correlated, or $p \to n$. Regularization trades a little bias for a large variance reduction.

---

## Section Overview

**The problem:** OLS breaks down when predictors are many, correlated, or when $p$ approaches $n$. Regularization adds a penalty that trades a little bias for a large variance reduction.

---

## OLS Instability with Many Predictors

**Recall OLS:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$

**Three failure modes in forecasting:**

1. **Near-multicollinearity** --- $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ is nearly singular; small data perturbations flip sign and magnitude of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$
2. **High dimensionality** --- with $p$ lags + rolling features + calendar dummies, $p$ can approach or exceed $n$
3. **Overfitting** --- OLS minimizes in-sample RSS exactly; generalization to new periods is poor

::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
RSXFS Feature Matrix
:::
With 12 lags + 3 rolling windows + 12 month dummies = 27 predictors on $n \approx 300$ monthly observations. Small by ML standards, but already enough for OLS instability with correlated lag features.
:::

---

## The Bias--Variance View

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="54%"}
**OLS is unbiased but high-variance:**

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\mathrm{OLS}}] = \boldsymbol{\beta} \quad \text{but} \quad \mathrm{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\mathrm{OLS}}) \text{ large}$$

**Regularized estimator accepts bias:**

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\lambda}] \neq \boldsymbol{\beta} \quad \text{but} \quad \mathrm{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\lambda}) \text{ smaller}$$

Net effect: **lower MSE** in finite samples when variance reduction exceeds the squared-bias increase.
:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
::: {.keybox}
**Bias--Variance Decomposition**

$\mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{Bias}^2 + \mathrm{Var} + \sigma^2$

Regularization shifts the tradeoff leftward along the *model complexity* axis. [@Hastie2009]
:::
:::

::::

---

## Penalized Regression: General Form

All penalized regression methods solve:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_\lambda = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \underbrace{\|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2}_{\text{fit (RSS)}} + \lambda \cdot \underbrace{P(\boldsymbol{\beta})}_{\text{penalty}}$$

::: {.smaller}

| **Method** | **Penalty** $P(\boldsymbol{\beta})$ | **Key property** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Ridge | $\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2 = \sum_j \beta_j^2$ | Shrinks, never zeros |
| LASSO | $\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_1 = \sum_j |\beta_j|$ | Shrinks + selects |
| Elastic Net | $\alpha\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_1 + (1-\alpha)\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2$ | Both |

:::

::: {.neutral .smaller}
$\lambda \geq 0$ controls penalty strength; $\lambda=0$ recovers OLS; $\lambda \to \infty$ shrinks $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \to \mathbf{0}$.
Elastic Net $\alpha$ is the L1/L2 mixing ratio --- distinct from ETS smoothing $\alpha$ (L03) and ECM speed-of-adjustment $\alpha$ (L05).
Tuning of $\lambda$ (and $\alpha$ for Elastic Net) is covered in Section 5.
:::

---

# Ridge Regression (L2)

Shrinks all coefficients toward zero; handles multicollinearity with a closed-form solution.

---

## Section Overview

**Ridge regression** adds an L2 penalty that shrinks all coefficients toward zero but never sets them exactly to zero. It has an analytical solution and handles multicollinearity well.

---

## Ridge: Objective and Solution

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Ridge Regression
:::
$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\mathrm{R}}_\lambda = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2 + \lambda \|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2$$

**Analytical solution:**
$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\mathrm{R}}_\lambda = \left(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I}\right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$$
:::

**Why does this fix near-singularity?**

- $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ may be near-singular (smallest eigenvalue $\approx 0$)
- Adding $\lambda\mathbf{I}$ shifts all eigenvalues up by $\lambda$: matrix becomes safely invertible [@HoerlKennard1970]
- Coefficients shrink by factor $d_j^2 / (d_j^2 + \lambda)$ along each principal direction $j$ (SVD interpretation)

---

## Ridge: Constraint Set Geometry

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Equivalent constrained form:**

$$\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \|\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2 \quad \text{s.t.}\ \|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2 \leq t$$

The Ridge constraint set is a **sphere** (circle in 2D). The OLS solution is usually outside; the Ridge solution is the point where the RSS ellipses first touch the sphere.

::: {.warningbox}
Coefficients are **never exactly zero** --- the sphere has no corners. Ridge does *not* perform variable selection.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
```{=html}
<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 260 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:240px; display:block; margin:0 auto;">
  <!-- Constraint circle -->
  <circle cx="105" cy="130" r="72" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="105" y="215" font-size="11" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="middle">‖β‖₂² ≤ t</text>
  <!-- Axes -->
  <line x1="20" y1="130" x2="240" y2="130" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1"/>
  <polygon points="238,126 246,130 238,134" fill="#aaa"/>
  <text x="248" y="134" font-size="10" fill="#aaa">β₁</text>
  <line x1="105" y1="240" x2="105" y2="20" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1"/>
  <polygon points="101,22 105,14 109,22" fill="#aaa"/>
  <text x="108" y="16" font-size="10" fill="#aaa">β₂</text>
  <!-- OLS solution -->
  <circle cx="195" cy="65" r="4" fill="#E41C38"/>
  <text x="200" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#E41C38">OLS β̂</text>
  <!-- RSS ellipses (3 concentric around OLS) -->
  <ellipse cx="195" cy="65" rx="36" ry="25" fill="none" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <ellipse cx="195" cy="65" rx="68" ry="47" fill="none" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <ellipse cx="195" cy="65" rx="105" ry="73" fill="none" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <!-- Ridge solution on circle boundary -->
  <circle cx="156" cy="79" r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <text x="161" y="76" font-size="10" fill="#005CA9">Ridge β̂ᴿ</text>
</svg>
```
:::

::::

---

## Ridge: Coefficient Path

As $\lambda$ increases from 0 to $\infty$, all coefficients shrink *smoothly* toward zero:

```{=html}
<svg width="80%" viewBox="0 0 480 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:220px; display:block; margin:0 auto;">
  <!-- Axes -->
  <line x1="50" y1="200" x2="450" y2="200" stroke="#1a1a1a" stroke-width="2"/>
  <polygon points="448,196 456,200 448,204" fill="#1a1a1a"/>
  <text x="460" y="204" font-size="12" fill="#1a1a1a">log₁₀(λ)</text>
  <line x1="50" y1="210" x2="50" y2="15" stroke="#1a1a1a" stroke-width="2"/>
  <polygon points="46,17 50,9 54,17" fill="#1a1a1a"/>
  <text x="18" y="12" font-size="12" fill="#1a1a1a">β̂ⱼ</text>
  <!-- Zero line -->
  <line x1="50" y1="120" x2="450" y2="120" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <!-- 5 coefficient paths, smooth curves shrinking to 0 -->
  <!-- β1: large positive (blue) -->
  <path d="M50,50 C120,55 200,90 420,118" fill="none" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <!-- β2: moderate positive (red) -->
  <path d="M50,75 C130,80 210,102 420,119" fill="none" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <!-- β3: negative (green) -->
  <path d="M50,185 C120,178 200,150 420,122" fill="none" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <!-- β4: small positive (gray) -->
  <path d="M50,95 C140,98 220,108 420,119" fill="none" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <!-- β5: moderate negative (purple) -->
  <path d="M50,170 C110,162 190,143 420,121" fill="none" stroke="#7c3aed" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <!-- OLS label at left -->
  <text x="28" y="123" font-size="10" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end">OLS</text>
  <!-- Lambda* vertical dashed line -->
  <line x1="250" y1="205" x2="250" y2="20" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="5,3"/>
  <text x="253" y="17" font-size="10" fill="#525252">λ* (CV)</text>
  <!-- → 0 label -->
  <text x="425" y="116" font-size="10" fill="#525252">→ 0</text>
</svg>
```

[*Socratic: if two predictors are perfectly correlated, what does Ridge do to their coefficients? What does LASSO do? (Answered in the next section.)*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## Ridge: Key Properties Summary

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Strengths:**

- Closed-form solution --- fast computation
- Handles multicollinearity: correlated predictors get *equal* shrinkage (spread out penalty)
- Continuous, stable in $\lambda$
- Works when $p > n$
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Limitations:**

- **No variable selection** --- all $p$ predictors remain in model
- Interpretation harder with many near-zero (but non-zero) coefficients
- Requires **standardized** predictors (or use sklearn `Pipeline` with `StandardScaler`)
:::

::::

::: {.keybox}
**When to use Ridge:** when you believe *all* predictors contribute a little (dense signal), or when predictors come in highly correlated groups.
:::

---

# LASSO Regression (L1)

Shrinks *and* selects: L1 penalty sets irrelevant coefficients to exactly zero.

---

## Section Overview

**LASSO** (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) uses an L1 penalty that both shrinks coefficients *and* sets some exactly to zero. It performs automatic variable selection [@Tibshirani1996].

---

## LASSO: Objective and Sparsity

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
LASSO Regression
:::
$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\mathrm{L}}_\lambda = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2 + \lambda \|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_1$$

$$\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_1 = \sum_{j=1}^{p} |\beta_j|$$
:::

**No closed form** --- solved by **coordinate descent**: update one $\beta_j$ at a time, applying *soft-thresholding*:

$$\hat{\beta}_j \leftarrow \mathrm{sign}(z_j)\,\max\!\left(|z_j| - \tfrac{\lambda}{2},\, 0\right)$$

where $z_j$ is the partial-residual inner product for predictor $j$. When $|z_j| \leq \lambda/2$, coefficient is set **exactly to zero**.

::: {.smaller}
**Example** ($\lambda=2$, threshold $=1$): $z_j = 1.8 \Rightarrow \hat{\beta}_j = +0.8$; $z_j = 0.7 \Rightarrow \hat{\beta}_j = 0$ [(zeroed out)]{.neutral}.
:::

---

## LASSO: Constraint Set Geometry

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Equivalent constrained form:**

$$\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \|\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2 \quad \text{s.t.}\ \|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_1 \leq t$$

The LASSO constraint set is a **diamond** (rotated square in 2D). The RSS ellipses typically first touch the diamond at a **corner**, where one or more $\beta_j = 0$ exactly.

::: {.keybox}
The corners of the $\ell_1$ ball are the source of sparsity. In $p$ dimensions: exponentially many corners at coordinate axes.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
```{=html}
<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 260 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:240px; display:block; margin:0 auto;">
  <!-- Diamond constraint set -->
  <polygon points="105,58 177,130 105,202 33,130" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="105" y="222" font-size="11" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="middle">‖β‖₁ ≤ t</text>
  <!-- Axes -->
  <line x1="20" y1="130" x2="240" y2="130" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1"/>
  <polygon points="238,126 246,130 238,134" fill="#aaa"/>
  <text x="248" y="134" font-size="10" fill="#aaa">β₁</text>
  <line x1="105" y1="240" x2="105" y2="20" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1"/>
  <polygon points="101,22 105,14 109,22" fill="#aaa"/>
  <text x="108" y="16" font-size="10" fill="#aaa">β₂</text>
  <!-- OLS solution -->
  <circle cx="193" cy="67" r="4" fill="#E41C38"/>
  <text x="198" y="64" font-size="10" fill="#E41C38">OLS β̂</text>
  <!-- RSS ellipses -->
  <ellipse cx="193" cy="67" rx="36" ry="25" fill="none" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <ellipse cx="193" cy="67" rx="68" ry="47" fill="none" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <ellipse cx="193" cy="67" rx="105" ry="73" fill="none" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <!-- LASSO solution at right corner (β₂=0) -->
  <circle cx="177" cy="130" r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <text x="182" y="127" font-size="10" fill="#005CA9">LASSO β̂ᴸ</text>
  <text x="182" y="142" font-size="10" fill="#525252">(β̂₂ = 0)</text>
</svg>
```
:::

::::

---

## LASSO: Coefficient Path

As $\lambda$ increases, LASSO coefficients shrink and hit zero at different $\lambda$ values:

```{=html}
<svg width="80%" viewBox="0 0 480 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:220px; display:block; margin:0 auto;">
  <!-- Axes -->
  <line x1="50" y1="200" x2="450" y2="200" stroke="#1a1a1a" stroke-width="2"/>
  <polygon points="448,196 456,200 448,204" fill="#1a1a1a"/>
  <text x="460" y="204" font-size="12" fill="#1a1a1a">log₁₀(λ)</text>
  <line x1="50" y1="210" x2="50" y2="15" stroke="#1a1a1a" stroke-width="2"/>
  <polygon points="46,17 50,9 54,17" fill="#1a1a1a"/>
  <text x="18" y="12" font-size="12" fill="#1a1a1a">β̂ⱼ</text>
  <!-- Zero line -->
  <line x1="50" y1="120" x2="450" y2="120" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <!-- β1: large positive (blue) — last to zero out -->
  <polyline points="50,50 120,68 178,90 250,115 330,120" fill="none" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <line x1="330" y1="120" x2="450" y2="120" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <!-- β2: moderate positive (red) -->
  <polyline points="50,75 115,88 168,103 214,120" fill="none" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <line x1="214" y1="120" x2="450" y2="120" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <!-- β3: negative, zeroed early (green) -->
  <polyline points="50,180 100,165 130,120" fill="none" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <line x1="130" y1="120" x2="450" y2="120" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <!-- β4: small positive, zeroed early (gray) -->
  <polyline points="50,98 82,107 105,120" fill="none" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <line x1="105" y1="120" x2="450" y2="120" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <!-- β5: moderate negative (purple) -->
  <polyline points="50,165 90,150 130,132 178,120" fill="none" stroke="#7c3aed" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <line x1="178" y1="120" x2="450" y2="120" stroke="#7c3aed" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <!-- OLS label -->
  <text x="28" y="123" font-size="10" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end">OLS</text>
  <!-- Lambda* vertical dashed line -->
  <line x1="280" y1="205" x2="280" y2="20" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="5,3"/>
  <text x="283" y="17" font-size="10" fill="#525252">λ* (CV)</text>
</svg>
```

[*Note the kinks (piecewise-linear path) --- a consequence of coordinate descent and the L1 geometry. Ridge paths are smooth curves.*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## LASSO: Properties and Limitations

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Strengths:**

- **Automatic variable selection** --- irrelevant lags zeroed out
- Interpretable: small active set survives
- Works when $p \gg n$
- Coefficient path is a diagnostic: shows which features enter first
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Limitations:**

- **Grouped predictors problem** --- among correlated features, LASSO picks one arbitrarily and zeros others
- Non-unique solution when $p > n$
- Slower than Ridge (no closed form)
- Sensitive to feature scaling (must standardize)
:::

::::

::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
RSXFS Application
:::
With 12 monthly lags, LASSO typically retains lags 1, 3, 12 and zeros lags 4--11 --- consistent with retail seasonality and recency effects. Rolling-window features may or may not survive depending on $\lambda^*$.
:::

---

# Elastic Net

Combines L1 and L2 penalties: sparsity from LASSO, grouped selection from Ridge.

---

## Section Overview

**Elastic Net** combines L1 and L2 penalties to get the best of both: sparsity from LASSO and grouped selection from Ridge. It uses two hyperparameters: $\lambda$ (overall strength) and $\alpha$ (mix ratio) [@ZouHastie2005].

[$\alpha$ here is the L1/L2 mixing parameter --- distinct from the level-smoothing $\alpha$ in ETS (Lecture 03) and the ECM speed-of-adjustment $\alpha$ in Lecture 05.]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## Elastic Net: Objective and Geometry

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Elastic Net
:::
$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\mathrm{EN}}_{\lambda,\alpha} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2 + \lambda \left[\alpha \|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_1 + (1-\alpha)\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2\right]$$

- $\alpha = 1$: pure LASSO
- $\alpha = 0$: pure Ridge
- $0 < \alpha < 1$: Elastic Net (interpolates between them)
:::

**Grouped selection property:** when predictors are correlated, Elastic Net tends to include or exclude them as a group --- unlike LASSO which arbitrarily picks one.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Two hyperparameters to tune:**

- $\lambda$ (penalty magnitude): grid search via CV
- $\alpha$ (mix): try $\{0.1, 0.5, 0.9\}$ or use `ElasticNetCV`
:::

::: {.column width="46%"}
::: {.warningbox}
Tuning both $\lambda$ and $\alpha$ simultaneously is expensive. Start with fixed $\alpha=0.5$ and tune $\lambda$ only.
:::
:::

::::

---

## Ridge vs. LASSO vs. Elastic Net: Decision Guide

::: {.smaller}

| **Situation** | **Ridge** | **LASSO** | **Elastic Net** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Dense signal (all $\beta_j \neq 0$) | Best | Tends to over-zero | Good |
| Sparse signal (few true features) | Over-retains | Best | Good |
| Correlated predictors (lag features) | Good (equal shrinkage) | Picks one; drops rest | Best |
| $p > n$ | Works | Selects $\leq n$ features | Works |
| Interpretability | Moderate | High (sparse) | Moderate |

:::

[*Socratic: in forecasting with 12 monthly lags, why might Elastic Net outperform pure LASSO? (Hint: are lags 1 and 2 correlated?)*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

# Tuning $\lambda$ via Cross-Validation

Select $\lambda^*$ with `TimeSeriesSplit` to respect temporal ordering.

---

## Section Overview

**Goal:** select $\lambda^*$ that minimizes out-of-sample prediction error. For time series, we must use `TimeSeriesSplit` (not random k-fold) to respect the temporal ordering of observations.

---

## TimeSeriesSplit CV for Regularization

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
**Procedure:**

1. Define $\lambda$ grid: e.g., $10^{-3}$ to $10^{3}$ (50 points, log-spaced)
2. For each $\lambda$:
   - Run `TimeSeriesSplit` with $K=5$ folds
   - Fit regularized model on each train fold
   - Record validation RMSE
3. Select $\lambda^*$ with lowest mean validation RMSE
4. Refit on train+val with $\lambda^*$; evaluate on test

::: {.warningbox}
**Never** fit the scaler (`StandardScaler`) on the full data before CV splits --- this constitutes data leakage. Use `sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline`.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
```{=html}
<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 310 230" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:220px; display:block; margin:0 auto;">
  <!-- F1 -->
  <text x="18" y="28" font-size="10" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="end">F1</text>
  <rect x="20" y="14" width="80" height="22" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8" rx="2"/>
  <rect x="100" y="14" width="34" height="22" fill="#D4EDDA" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="0.8" rx="2"/>
  <!-- F2 -->
  <text x="18" y="60" font-size="10" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="end">F2</text>
  <rect x="20" y="46" width="114" height="22" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8" rx="2"/>
  <rect x="134" y="46" width="34" height="22" fill="#D4EDDA" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="0.8" rx="2"/>
  <!-- F3 -->
  <text x="18" y="92" font-size="10" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="end">F3</text>
  <rect x="20" y="78" width="148" height="22" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8" rx="2"/>
  <rect x="168" y="78" width="34" height="22" fill="#D4EDDA" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="0.8" rx="2"/>
  <!-- F4 -->
  <text x="18" y="124" font-size="10" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="end">F4</text>
  <rect x="20" y="110" width="182" height="22" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8" rx="2"/>
  <rect x="202" y="110" width="34" height="22" fill="#D4EDDA" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="0.8" rx="2"/>
  <!-- F5 -->
  <text x="18" y="156" font-size="10" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="end">F5</text>
  <rect x="20" y="142" width="216" height="22" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8" rx="2"/>
  <rect x="236" y="142" width="34" height="22" fill="#D4EDDA" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="0.8" rx="2"/>
  <!-- Legend -->
  <rect x="20" y="178" width="14" height="10" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.7"/>
  <text x="37" y="187" font-size="10" fill="#005CA9">Train</text>
  <rect x="90" y="178" width="14" height="10" fill="#D4EDDA" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="0.7"/>
  <text x="107" y="187" font-size="10" fill="#15803d">Val</text>
  <!-- Time axis -->
  <line x1="20" y1="200" x2="290" y2="200" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <polygon points="288,196 296,200 288,204" fill="#525252"/>
  <text x="298" y="204" font-size="10" fill="#525252" font-style="italic">time</text>
</svg>
```
:::

::::

---

## Validation Curve: Selecting $\lambda^*$

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
**Plot validation RMSE vs. $\log_{10}(\lambda)$:**

```{=html}
<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 320 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:230px; display:block; margin:0 auto;">
  <!-- Axes -->
  <line x1="50" y1="200" x2="300" y2="200" stroke="#1a1a1a" stroke-width="2"/>
  <polygon points="298,196 306,200 298,204" fill="#1a1a1a"/>
  <text x="310" y="204" font-size="11" fill="#1a1a1a">log₁₀(λ)</text>
  <line x1="50" y1="210" x2="50" y2="20" stroke="#1a1a1a" stroke-width="2"/>
  <polygon points="46,22 50,14 54,22" fill="#1a1a1a"/>
  <text x="22" y="18" font-size="11" fill="#1a1a1a">RMSE</text>
  <!-- U-shaped validation curve (blue) -->
  <path d="M60,170 C90,120 120,80 170,72 C210,75 250,110 285,170" fill="none" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <!-- Train curve (dashed gray, monotone decreasing) -->
  <path d="M60,145 C100,100 150,65 285,40" fill="none" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.8" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/>
  <!-- Optimal lambda marker -->
  <line x1="170" y1="200" x2="170" y2="73" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <circle cx="170" cy="72" r="4" fill="#E41C38"/>
  <text x="175" y="68" font-size="11" fill="#E41C38">λ*</text>
  <!-- Labels -->
  <text x="230" y="115" font-size="11" fill="#005CA9">Val</text>
  <text x="230" y="58" font-size="11" fill="#525252">Train</text>
  <!-- Region labels -->
  <text x="82" y="190" font-size="10" fill="#525252">overfit</text>
  <text x="230" y="155" font-size="10" fill="#525252">underfit</text>
</svg>
```
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
**Reading the curve:**

- **Left of $\lambda^*$**: low $\lambda$ $\Rightarrow$ low bias, high variance $\Rightarrow$ overfit (train $\ll$ val)
- **Right of $\lambda^*$**: high $\lambda$ $\Rightarrow$ high bias, low variance $\Rightarrow$ underfit (both high)
- **At $\lambda^*$**: optimal bias--variance tradeoff

::: {.keybox}
**Practical rule:** *one standard error rule* --- pick the largest $\lambda$ within 1 SE of the minimum (slightly more regularized, more robust).
:::
:::

::::

[*When would the one-SE rule backfire? (Hint: consider a highly asymmetric validation curve.)*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

# Application to Forecasting

Ridge, LASSO, and Elastic Net on RSXFS retail sales vs. SARIMA baseline.

---

## Section Overview

Apply Ridge, LASSO, and Elastic Net to the RSXFS retail sales series. Use a leakage-free sklearn `Pipeline` and evaluate on a held-out test set against the SARIMA baseline.

---

## sklearn Pipeline for Leakage-Free CV

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
```python
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge, Lasso
from sklearn.model_selection import (
    TimeSeriesSplit, GridSearchCV)
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

# Build pipeline (scaler fitted INSIDE CV)
pipe = Pipeline([
    ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
    ('model',  Ridge())
])

tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5, gap=0)
# sklearn 'alpha' = our lambda (penalty strength)
# (for ElasticNet: 'l1_ratio' = our alpha mix)
param_grid = {'model__alpha':
    np.logspace(-3, 3, 60)}

gs = GridSearchCV(pipe, param_grid,
    cv=tscv,
    scoring='neg_root_mean_squared_error',
    refit=True)
gs.fit(X_trainval, y_trainval)
```
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
```python
# Evaluate on held-out test set
y_pred = gs.best_estimator_.predict(X_test)
rmse_test = np.sqrt(
    mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred))
print(f"Best alpha: {gs.best_params_}")
print(f"Test RMSE:  {rmse_test:.2f}")

# Inspect coefficients
coef = gs.best_estimator_.named_steps[
    'model'].coef_
feat_names = X_trainval.columns.tolist()
pd.Series(coef, index=feat_names)\
  .sort_values().plot.barh()
```

::: {.keybox}
**Key:** `StandardScaler` is inside the pipeline. It fits on the train fold only during CV, preventing leakage.
:::
:::

::::

---

## Interpreting LASSO Coefficients in Forecasting

**What survives LASSO regularization on RSXFS?**

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
**Typical surviving features** (at $\lambda^*$):

- **Lag 1** ($y_{t-1}$) --- strongest short-run predictor
- **Lag 12** ($y_{t-12}$) --- seasonal anchor (same month, prior year)
- **Lag 3** ($y_{t-3}$) --- quarterly momentum
- **Rolling mean 12** --- trend level
- **December dummy** --- holiday retail spike

**Typically zeroed:**

- Lags 4--11 (redundant with lag 1 + lag 12)
- Rolling std (noisy; insufficient sample)
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Interpretation Rule
:::
A LASSO coefficient of zero means the feature adds no predictive value *after* accounting for all other active features. It does not mean the feature is uncorrelated with $y_t$ in isolation.
:::

[*Compare to ARIMA: ARIMA implicitly uses all lags up to order $p$; LASSO selects the most predictive subset, potentially skipping lags.*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::::

---

## Forecast Comparison: SARIMA vs. Regularized Models

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
**Typical results on RSXFS (24-month test set):**

::: {.smaller}

| **Model** | **RMSE** | **MAE** |
|:---|---:|---:|
| Seasonal Naïve | 4,210 | 3,120 |
| SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)₁₂ | 2,840 | 2,100 |
| Ridge ($\lambda^*$) | 2,680 | 1,980 |
| LASSO ($\lambda^*$) | 2,590 | 1,910 |
| Elastic Net ($\lambda^*$) | 2,540 | 1,890 |

:::

[*Values are illustrative (actual results may vary with feature set and sample period).*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
**Takeaways:**

- All regularized models beat SARIMA on this feature set
- Elastic Net has a small edge --- lag features are correlated
- Gains are modest ($\approx$5--10%) --- SARIMA already captures most AR and seasonal structure
- Larger gains expected in **multi-series** settings (shared regularization) or when many external regressors exist
:::

::::

---

# Takeaways and References

---

## Lecture 8 Key Takeaways

::: {.keybox}
1. **OLS instability** with many/correlated predictors motivates regularization --- the bias--variance tradeoff at work.
2. **Ridge** (L2) shrinks all coefficients smoothly; no variable selection. Best for dense signals or highly correlated groups.
3. **LASSO** (L1) shrinks *and* zeros coefficients; performs automatic variable selection. Best for sparse signals.
4. **Elastic Net** combines L1+L2; handles correlated features better than pure LASSO (grouped selection property).
5. **Tune $\lambda$ via TimeSeriesSplit CV** inside a `Pipeline` to prevent data leakage --- this is non-negotiable for time series.
:::

**Preview of Lecture 09:** Tree-Based Methods --- Random Forests and XGBoost capture nonlinearities that penalized linear models cannot.

---

## References {.smaller}

::: {#refs}
:::
